template: Find the mean of: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}, {{e}}
Find the median of: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}, {{e}}
explanation: Order the values: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}, {{e}}. With 5 values, the median is the 3rd value = {{c}}.

template: Find the median of: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}
explanation: Order: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}. With 4 values, median = mean of 2nd and 3rd = ({{b}} + {{c}}) ÷ 2 = {{med}}.
The mode is the value that appears most often. {{m}} appears twice; all others appear once. Mode = {{m}}.

template: Find the range of: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}, {{e}}
explanation: Range = largest − smallest = {{e}} − {{a}} = {{rng}}.

template: A frequency table shows scores: {{v1}} appears {{f1}} times, {{v2}} appears {{f2}} times, {{v3}} appears {{f3}} times. Find the mean.
Grouped data: 0–9 (freq {{f1}}), 10–19 (freq {{f2}}), 20–29 (freq {{f3}}). Using midpoints, estimate the mean.
A bag contains {{r}} red, {{b}} blue and {{g}} green balls. A ball is chosen at random. What is the probability it is red?
explanation: Total balls = {{r}} + {{b}} + {{g}} = {{total}}. P(red) = {{r}}/{{total}}{{gcd_rg > 1 ? ' = ' + pnum + '/' + pden : ''}}.

template: The probability that it rains tomorrow is {{p}}. What is the probability that it does NOT rain?
explanation: P(not rain) = 1 − P(rain) = 1 − {{pnum}}/{{pden}} = {{qnum}}/{{pden}}.

template: Events A and B are mutually exclusive. P(A) = {{pa}}/{{d}} and P(B) = {{pb}}/{{d}}. Find P(A or B).
explanation: For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) = {{pa}}/{{d}} + {{pb}}/{{d}} = {{psum}}/{{d}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: A coin is flipped {{n}} times and lands heads {{h}} times. What is the relative frequency of heads?
explanation: Relative frequency = frequency ÷ total trials = {{h}} ÷ {{n}} = {{rf}}.

template: A biased die has P(6) = {{pnum}}/{{pden}}. The die is rolled {{n}} times. How many times would you expect to get a 6?
explanation: Expected frequency = probability × number of trials = ({{pnum}}/{{pden}}) × {{n}} = {{exp}}.

template: A bag has {{r}} red and {{b}} blue balls. Two balls are drawn with replacement. Find P(both red).
explanation: With replacement, trials are independent. P(red) = {{r}}/{{total}} each time. P(both red) = ({{r}}/{{total}})² = {{rawNum}}/{{rawDen}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: A bag has {{r}} red and {{b}} blue balls. Two balls are drawn without replacement. Find P(both red).
explanation: Without replacement: P(1st red) = {{r}}/{{total}}, P(2nd red | 1st red) = {{r-1}}/{{total-1}}. P(both red) = ({{r}}/{{total}}) × ({{r-1}}/{{total-1}}) = {{rawNum}}/{{rawDen}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: P(A) = {{pa}}/{{d}} and P(B) = {{pb}}/{{d}} are independent events. Find P(A and B).
explanation: Independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) = ({{pa}}/{{d}}) × ({{pb}}/{{d}}) = {{rawNum}}/{{rawDen}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: In a class of {{total}} students: {{a}} study French, {{b}} study Spanish, and {{both}} study both. How many study French or Spanish?
explanation: Using the addition rule: n(F ∪ S) = n(F) + n(S) − n(F ∩ S) = {{a}} + {{b}} − {{both}} = {{union}}.

template: In a group of {{total}} people, {{a}} like tea, {{b}} like coffee, and {{both}} like both. A person is chosen at random. Find P(likes tea only).

botAns.expr: (onlyT + onlyC + both + 3) / gcd(onlyT, onlyT + onlyC + both + 3)

explanation: Tea only = {{a}} − {{both}} = {{onlyT}}. P(tea only) = {{onlyT}}/{{total}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: A scatter diagram shows that as temperature increases, ice cream sales increase. What type of correlation is this?
explanation: When both variables increase together, this is positive correlation. The scatter points rise from left to right.

template: A scatter diagram shows that as the age of a car increases, its value decreases. What type of correlation is this?
explanation: When one variable increases as the other decreases, this is negative correlation. The scatter points fall from left to right.

template: A line of best fit has equation y = {{m}}x + {{c}}. Predict y when x = {{xv}}.
A cumulative frequency graph has {{n}} data values. At what cumulative frequency does the median occur?
explanation: The median is at the n/2 th value in a cumulative frequency graph. For {{n}} values, read off at cumulative frequency = {{n}} ÷ 2 = {{med}}.

template: The lower quartile is {{lq}} and the upper quartile is {{uq}}. Find the interquartile range.
explanation: IQR = upper quartile − lower quartile = {{uq}} − {{lq}} = {{iqr}}.

template: A data set has {{n}} values arranged in order. At what position is the lower quartile?
explanation: Lower quartile (Q1) is at the n/4 th value. For {{n}} values: position = {{n}} ÷ 4 = {{lqpos}}.

template: A box plot shows: minimum {{mn}}, lower quartile {{lq}}, median {{med}}, upper quartile {{uq}}, maximum {{mx}}. Find the interquartile range.
explanation: IQR = UQ − LQ = {{uq}} − {{lq}} = {{iqr}}.

template: Group A has median {{medA}} and IQR {{iqrA}}. Group B has median {{medB}} and IQR {{iqrB}}. Which group has greater consistency?
explanation: A smaller IQR means less spread, so greater consistency. Group B has IQR = {{iqrB}}, which is smaller than Group A's IQR of {{iqrA}}, so Group B is more consistent.

template: A histogram bar has class width {{w}} and frequency density {{fd}}. Find the frequency.
explanation: Frequency = frequency density × class width = {{fd}} × {{w}} = {{freq}}.

template: A class interval has width {{w}} and frequency {{freq}}. What is the frequency density?
explanation: Frequency density = frequency ÷ class width = {{freq}} ÷ {{w}} = {{fd}}.

template: A pie chart represents {{total}} items. One category has {{n}} items. What angle (in degrees) should its sector be?
explanation: Angle = (n/total) × 360° = ({{n}}/{{total}}) × 360° = {{angle}}°.

template: A pie chart represents {{total}} people. A sector has an angle of {{angle}}°. How many people does it represent?
explanation: Frequency = (angle/360) × total = ({{angle}}/360) × {{total}} = {{freq}}.

template: A school has {{n1}} Year 10 students and {{n2}} Year 11 students. A stratified sample of {{sampleSize}} is taken. How many Year 10 students should be in the sample?
explanation: Proportion of Year 10 = {{n1}}/{{total}}. Year 10 in sample = ({{n1}}/{{total}}) × {{sampleSize}} = {{s1}}.

template: Every 10th person on a list is selected for a survey. What sampling method is this?
explanation: Selecting every nth item from an ordered list is called systematic sampling.

template: In a two-way table: {{mpass}} males passed, {{mfail}} males failed, {{fpass}} females passed, {{ffail}} females failed. How many people passed in total?
explanation: Total passed = males who passed + females who passed = {{mpass}} + {{fpass}} = {{totalPass}}.

template: {{mpass}} males passed, {{mfail}} males failed. Given a person is male, find P(passed).
explanation: P(passed | male) = number of males who passed ÷ total males = {{mpass}}/{{totalM}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: A frequency table shows: score 1 (freq {{f1}}), score 2 (freq {{f2}}), score 3 (freq {{f3}}), score 4 (freq {{f4}}). What is the modal score?
explanation: The modal score is the one with the highest frequency. Score 3 has frequency {{f3}}, which is the highest. Mode = 3.

template: Frequency table: value 1 (freq {{f1}}), value 2 (freq {{f2}}), value 3 (freq {{f3}}). Find the median.
explanation: Total frequency = {{total}}. Median is at position ({{total}}+1)/2 = {{halfPos}}. Running totals: after value 1: {{f1}}, after value 2: {{f1+f2}}. The {{halfPos}}th value falls in value {{median}}.

template: A back-to-back stem-and-leaf diagram has {{n}} values in each group. At which position is the median in each group?
explanation: With {{n}} values, the median is at position (n+1)/2 = ({{n}}+1)/2 = {{pos}}.

template: P(event A) = {{pnum}}/{{pden}}. The trial is repeated {{reps}} times independently. Find P(A occurs at least once).
explanation: P(at least one A) = 1 − P(no A in {{reps}} trials) = 1 − ({{qnum}}/{{pden}})^{{reps}} = 1 − {{rawNum}}/{{rawDen}} = {{rawDen - rawNum}}/{{rawDen}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: Which of the following is an example of continuous data?

options: The height of students in cm
The number of books on a shelf
answerConfig.answer: The height of students in cm

explanation: Continuous data can take any value within a range and is measured (e.g. height, mass, time). Discrete data is counted in whole numbers.

template: Which of the following is an example of discrete data?

options: The number of cars in a car park
The length of a piece of string
answerConfig.answer: The number of cars in a car park

explanation: Discrete data can only take certain specific values, usually integers (e.g. counts). The number of cars must be a whole number.

template: A survey about library use is given only to people entering a library. Why might this produce biased results?

options: Only library users are surveyed, not the general population
answerConfig.answer: Only library users are surveyed, not the general population

explanation: The sample only includes library users, so it doesn't represent people who don't use libraries. This makes the sample biased — it over-represents library users.

template: Frequencies are: 0–10: {{f1}}, 10–20: {{f2}}, 20–30: {{f3}}. What is the cumulative frequency at the end of the 10–20 class?
explanation: Cumulative frequency after 10–20 class = f(0–10) + f(10–20) = {{f1}} + {{f2}} = {{cf2}}.

template: The mean of {{n}} values is {{mean}}. The first {{nm1}} values sum to {{partSum}}. Find the missing value.
explanation: Total sum = mean × n = {{mean}} × {{n}} = {{total}}. Missing value = total − partial sum = {{total}} − {{partSum}} = {{missing}}.

template: Place these events on a probability scale from 0 to 1. Which event has probability closest to 0?

options: Rolling a 1 on a standard fair die
Getting a head on a fair coin
It will be dark at midnight
A randomly chosen number from 1–10 is prime
answerConfig.answer: Rolling a 1 on a standard fair die

explanation: P(roll 1 on a die) = 1/6 ≈ 0.167, P(heads) = 0.5, P(dark at midnight) ≈ 1, P(prime from 1–10) = 4/10 = 0.4. Rolling a 1 has the smallest probability.

template: A coin is flipped and a {{n}}-sided die is rolled. How many possible outcomes are there?
explanation: Coin has 2 outcomes. Die has {{n}} outcomes. Total = 2 × {{n}} = {{outcomes}} outcomes.

template: Two fair dice are rolled. How many ways can the total be {{target}}?
Two fair dice are rolled. Find the probability that the total is {{target}}.
explanation: Total outcomes = 6 × 6 = 36. Favourable outcomes (total = {{target}}) = {{ways}}. P = {{ways}}/36{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: A frequency polygon plots points at the ___ of each class interval.
explanation: In a frequency polygon, the frequency is plotted against the midpoint of each class interval, then connected with straight lines.

template: A data set has LQ = {{lq}}, UQ = {{uq}}, IQR = {{iqr}}. Is the value {{val}} an outlier?
explanation: Upper fence = UQ + 1.5 × IQR = {{uq}} + 1.5 × {{iqr}} = {{uq + 1.5*iqr}}. Since {{val}} > {{uq + 1.5*iqr}}, it IS an outlier.

template: A bar chart has a y-axis starting at {{ystart}} instead of 0. Why could this be misleading?

options: It exaggerates the differences between bars
It makes all bars the same height
It reduces the differences between bars
answerConfig.answer: It exaggerates the differences between bars

explanation: Starting the y-axis at {{ystart}} rather than 0 cuts off the bottom of all bars, making relative differences appear much larger than they actually are. This is a misleading representation.

template: Calculate the 3-point moving average for the first group using values: {{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}.
explanation: First 3-point moving average = ({{a}} + {{b}} + {{c}}) ÷ 3 = {{a+b+c}} ÷ 3 = {{ma1}}.

template: A time series graph shows sales are always highest in December. What does this suggest?
explanation: A pattern that repeats at the same time each year is called seasonal variation. High December sales (e.g. Christmas) is a classic seasonal pattern.

template: Match each term to its correct definition.

pairs.right: Sum of values divided by count

explanation: Mean = sum÷count; Median = middle when ordered; Mode = most frequent; Range = max−min.

template: Order these events from least likely to most likely:

items: Rolling a 6 on a fair die (P = 1/6)
Getting tails on a fair coin (P = 1/2)
Picking a red card from a standard deck (P = 1/2)

explanation: 1/6 < 1/2 = 1/2 < 1. Order: rolling a 6 → tails/red card → sun rising. (Tails and red card have equal probability.)

template: A two-way table: {{mpass}} males passed, {{mfail}} males failed, {{fpass}} females passed, {{ffail}} females failed. Find P(female | passed).
explanation: P(female | passed) = females who passed ÷ total who passed = {{fpass}}/{{totalPass}}{{g > 1 ? ' = ' + topAns + '/' + botAns : ''}}.

template: In a normal distribution with mean {{mu}} and standard deviation {{sigma}}, approximately what percentage of data lies within one standard deviation of the mean?
explanation: In a normal distribution, approximately 68% of data lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean (between {{mu - sigma}} and {{mu + sigma}}).

template: Group A: mean = {{meanA}}, standard deviation = {{sdA}}. Group B: mean = {{meanB}}, standard deviation = {{sdB}}. Which group has greater spread?
explanation: A larger standard deviation means greater spread. Group B has SD = {{sdB}}, compared to Group A's SD = {{sdA}}, so Group B has greater spread.

template: A researcher wants to know the average height of all 1500 students in a school but measures only 50 of them. The 50 students are the ___.
explanation: The whole group being studied (all 1500 students) is the population. The subset that is actually measured (50 students) is the sample.

template: Grouped data: 0–4 (freq {{f1}}), 5–9 (freq {{f2}}), 10–14 (freq {{f3}}), 15–19 (freq {{f4}}). Estimate the mean.
P(A) = 1/2, P(B) = 1/3, P(A and B) = {{pab}}. Are A and B independent?
explanation: For independence: P(A and B) must equal P(A) × P(B) = 1/2 × 1/3 = 1/6. {{pab == '1/6' ? 'Since P(A and B) = 1/6, A and B ARE independent.' : 'Since P(A and B) = 1/4 ≠ 1/6, A and B are NOT independent.'}}

template: Are 'rolling an even number' and 'rolling a multiple of 3' on a standard die mutually exclusive?
explanation: For mutually exclusive events, they cannot both occur at the same time. Rolling a 6 is both even AND a multiple of 3, so they CAN occur simultaneously. They are NOT mutually exclusive.

template: A class interval is written as 20 ≤ x < 30. What are the class boundaries?
explanation: The class boundaries are the endpoints of the interval: lower boundary = 20 (included, ≤), upper boundary = 30 (excluded, <).

template: Which of the following are measures of spread?
explanation: Measures of spread describe how data is distributed. Range and IQR are measures of spread. Mean and mode are measures of average (central tendency).

template: P(A) = {{pa}}/{{d}}. Given A occurred, P(B|A) = {{pba}}/{{d}}. Given A did not occur, P(B|A') = {{pbna}}/{{d}}. Find P(B).
Find the lower quartile of the ordered data: {{v1}}, {{v2}}, {{v3}}, {{v4}}, {{v5}}, {{v6}}, {{v7}}, {{v8}}.
explanation: With 8 values, LQ is the mean of the 2nd and 3rd values: ({{v2}} + {{v3}}) / 2 = {{lq}}.

template: Two judges rank {{n}} competitors. The rank correlation is close to +1. What does this suggest?

options: The judges largely agree in their rankings
There is no relationship between the rankings
One judge always ranks higher than the other
answerConfig.answer: The judges largely agree in their rankings

explanation: A Spearman's rank correlation close to +1 means strong positive correlation — competitors ranked highly by one judge tend to be ranked highly by the other. The judges largely agree.

template: If P(A) = 0.{{x}}, then P(not A) = 0.{{comp}}. Is this correct?
explanation: P(not A) = 1 − P(A) = 1 − 0.{{x}} = 0.{{comp}}. This is correct.

template: A scatter graph has data from x = {{xmin}} to x = {{xmax}}. A student predicts y when x = {{xext}}. What type of prediction is this, and why is it unreliable?

options: Extrapolation — the prediction is outside the data range
Interpolation — the prediction is inside the data range
Extrapolation — the prediction is inside the data range
Interpolation — the prediction is outside the data range
answerConfig.answer: Extrapolation — the prediction is outside the data range

explanation: The data range is {{xmin}} to {{xmax}}. Predicting at x = {{xext}} goes beyond the data range — this is extrapolation. It is unreliable because the trend may not continue outside the observed range.
